skill.name: defi-mev-battletest
skill.description: Guidance for DeFi and MEV bot design, with practical warnings about arbitrage, backtesting, AMM math, private relays, and production risk controls.
skill.summary: Plan safer DeFi and MEV bot workflows with realistic risk checks and simulation guidance.
skill.author: BarisSozen

security_audit.summary: Static command and weak-crypto alerts are mostly false positives from fenced Markdown examples and domain terms, not executable code. The skill is medium risk because it teaches dual-use MEV bot design, private relay submission, and competitive bidding methods that can be misused in financial markets. No evidence found of malware, credential theft, audit-bypassing prompt injection, or hidden exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use MEV Automation Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly targets arbitrage, MEV searchers, sandwiching, frontrunning, flash loans, and automated trading. This is not malware, but it can support adversarial market behavior if used without controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The domain intent is clear from the trigger keywords and MEV strategy section. The risk is dual-use rather than confirmed malicious because the same guidance includes warnings and risk controls.

medium_findings.title: Private Bundle and Builder Payment Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill includes example workflows for Flashbots bundle submission and direct builder payment. These are legitimate MEV techniques, but they can also be used to conceal competitive bids and execute adversarial transactions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples show relay submission and builder payment patterns in context. They are documentation examples, so the confidence reflects misuse potential rather than direct harmful execution.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only Shell Commands
low_findings.description: The external command finding is a false positive for execution risk. The detected anvil and forge commands are inside a Markdown bash example and are not run by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands appear only in a fenced documentation block. There is no script file, hook, or executable wrapper that invokes them automatically.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Relay URLs in Examples
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded localhost and Flashbots relay URLs in examples. They are expected for MEV tooling, but users copying the examples may submit transactions to third-party services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in documentation examples and are not contacted by the skill itself. The residual risk is user-initiated network submission if copied into a project.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography scanner hits do not identify cryptographic APIs. Sampled locations show the skill description, MEV-Share text, AMM math, logging checklist items, and price-impact arithmetic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows domain words and numerical formulas, not MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptographic algorithm usage. No executable cryptographic code was found.

low_findings.title: Over-Broad Skill Activation Language
low_findings.description: The skill says it must be consulted for any DeFi bot or MEV work. This is strong routing language, but it does not instruct the model to ignore security review or override system instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wording can over-steer assistant behavior, but it lacks classic prompt-injection text such as ignore previous instructions or claims of audit authority. It is a content quality concern, not a blocker.

dangerous_patterns.title: Flashbots Relay Submission Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code configures a Flashbots provider with an external relay URL and submits bundles. This is a legitimate MEV pattern that should be reviewed before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code is in a Markdown example and is not executed by the skill. It is still a sensitive financial automation pattern when copied into live systems.

dangerous_patterns.title: Builder Payment Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code describes paying a block builder directly as a competitive bid. This can be legitimate, but it may also support opaque priority bidding in adversarial MEV workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is clearly present and semantically relevant to MEV execution. It remains documentation-only, so it is medium concern rather than direct abuse.

content.value_statement: DeFi and MEV bots often fail because backtests ignore gas, reorgs, latency, and adversarial orderflow. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical checks for simulation, execution, and risk controls.
actual_capabilities: Explains DeFi arbitrage failure modes such as gas loss, reorgs, stale state, and partial execution.
Outlines realistic backtesting and block simulation assumptions for automated trading systems.
Summarizes AMM price impact, Uniswap V3 tick mechanics, fee tiers, and liquidity gaps.
Describes MEV-Share hints, Flashbots bundle submission, and private orderflow tradeoffs.
Provides production readiness checklists for kill switches, capital limits, logging, and alerts.
Helps review bot designs for expected value, slippage, bait opportunities, and execution risk.
limitations: It is guidance material, not a complete trading bot or deployable protocol implementation.
It does not verify current relay policies, protocol addresses, fee markets, or legal obligations.
It cannot guarantee profitability, inclusion, or protection from competing searchers.
Examples must be adapted and security-reviewed before any live fund movement.

use_cases.title: Review a Bot Design Before Coding
use_cases.description: Map strategy, execution, and risk layers before building a DeFi bot that moves real funds.

use_cases.title: Stress-Test Backtest Assumptions
use_cases.description: Add realistic failure rates, gas uncertainty, stale state checks, and competition assumptions to simulations.

use_cases.title: Audit MEV Execution Workflows
use_cases.description: Check private relay, bundle simulation, slippage, and kill switch requirements before production deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Explain the Main Risks
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner overview for a new DeFi bot idea.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the defi-mev-battletest skill to explain the main risks in my DeFi arbitrage idea. Focus on gas, reorgs, slippage, stale state, and competition.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Backtest Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the defi-mev-battletest skill to review my backtest plan. Identify unrealistic assumptions and suggest simulation checks before live trading.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Production Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced pre-launch checklist for a bot.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the defi-mev-battletest skill to assess whether this MEV bot design is production ready. Check kill switches, capital limits, logging, private submission, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Model Expected Value Under Stress
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced risk modeling for a live strategy.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the defi-mev-battletest skill to model expected value for this strategy under gas spikes, reorgs, failed transactions, bait opportunities, and priority fee competition.

output_examples.input: Review a simple two-pool arbitrage bot idea.
output_examples.output: The review would flag stale state, failed transaction gas, competition, and missing kill switches. It would recommend pending-state simulation and strict capital limits.

output_examples.input: Check whether my Uniswap V3 slippage model is realistic.
output_examples.output: The response would explain tick liquidity, fee tier effects, liquidity gaps, and why linear slippage assumptions can understate losses.

output_examples.input: Prepare a pre-launch checklist for private bundle submission.
output_examples.output: The checklist would cover bundle simulation, relay behavior, profitability after gas, non-inclusion handling, alerts, and emergency stop procedures.

best_practices: Use the skill before writing trading code, then repeat the review before deploying with real funds.
Validate every copied example against current protocol docs, chain conditions, and legal requirements.
Treat profitable simulations as hypotheses until tested with realistic failure rates and small capital.
anti_patterns: Do not treat arbitrage as risk-free because a backtest shows positive historical returns.
Do not submit profitable transactions through public mempools without considering extraction risk.
Do not deploy without kill switches, loss limits, reorg handling, and complete failure logging.

faq.question: Does this skill build a complete MEV bot?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance, risk checks, and example concepts for reviewing bot architecture.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for beginners?
faq.answer: It is educational, but the domain is financially risky. Beginners should not deploy live trading systems from examples alone.

faq.question: Can it guarantee profitable arbitrage?
faq.answer: No. It emphasizes why many apparently profitable opportunities fail after gas, competition, and execution risk.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: The skill itself is Markdown content. Some examples reference relay URLs that users may call in their own projects.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The file is not malware, but it teaches dual-use MEV workflows that can be misused in adversarial trading.

faq.question: What should users verify before production use?
faq.answer: Users should verify protocol math, relay policies, legal obligations, private key handling, loss limits, and live chain conditions.
